one_liner: The pill in your hand becomes the helpline number.
search_doc:
The pill in your hand becomes the helpline number.
Mechanisms: everyday-object-transformation, code-as-content, purpose-driven-product, typographic-system
Idea: A helpline that reaches people in the most critical moment — when they have a pill in their hand. It turns the mandatory imprint codes on opioid pills into phone extensions (codeine IP33 dials as 4733, oxycodone K9 as 59); callers hear the story of someone who managed to quit that very pill, followed by live advice from experts.
Insight: The mandatory imprint codes stamped on every opioid pill — designed for medical traceability — can be reframed into a phone number, putting help directly in the addict's hand at the exact moment of relapse.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DESIGN/GOLD/855 How To Quit - Anzen Health (Service Plan Munich).jpg

extracted.tagline: The helpline that turns the object of addiction into a way out.
extracted.context: Every 6 minutes, someone in the United States dies from opioids. Opioids remain widely accessible for medical reasons; if people can't be prevented from getting pills, how can they be prevented from abusing them?
extracted.idea: A helpline that reaches people in the most critical moment — when they have a pill in their hand. It turns the mandatory imprint codes on opioid pills into phone extensions (codeine IP33 dials as 4733, oxycodone K9 as 59); callers hear the story of someone who managed to quit that very pill, followed by live advice from experts.
execution: Contextual OOH advertising (billboards, bus shelters, benches)
Social media activist posts
PSA on national TV
Website with more information
Web design inspired by grid layout of prescription bottle labels
Hyper-realistic 3D pill renders across all media
Custom typeface with ligatures
metrics: 142 people wanting to share their own stories of recovery

inferred.insight: The mandatory imprint codes stamped on every opioid pill — designed for medical traceability — can be reframed into a phone number, putting help directly in the addict's hand at the exact moment of relapse.
inferred.one_liner: The pill in your hand becomes the helpline number.
inferred.qualitative_summary: An integrated identity system turns regulatory pill imprints into phone extensions, reaching 137M Americans in 4 weeks and producing 8,000+ helpline calls plus 142 recovery-story volunteers.

visual.style_description: Editorial case-board layout centred on a hero billboard photograph showing the giant '855-HOW-TO-QUIT-( )' headline with real coloured pills arranged inside the parentheses; supporting strip on the right shows a smartphone interface and a tiled grid of differently coloured pills; left column carries dense body text, press logos and a thumbnail strip of OOH placements runs along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Reframing the mandatory FDA imprint codes stamped on every opioid pill as the phone extensions of a recovery helpline is a category-defining design idea — a regulated, machine-readable object becomes a piece of typography that calls for help. The conceptual one-liner ('the pill in your hand is the number to dial') is instantly memorable and natively typographic, which is exactly the kind of idea the Design jury rewards.

strategy.rationale: Anzen Health is a US health-services consultancy positioning itself around addiction-recovery support, so building a helpline product is direct mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The cited context ('every 6 minutes someone in the US dies from opioids') is a widely reported CDC-aligned cultural fact, and the insight that pill imprint codes can be read as dialable digits is verifiable on any pill in circulation. The board would be stronger with a sourced figure on relapse-moment intervention, but the strategic frame holds.

execution.rationale: The craft is a full identity system, not a one-off: a custom typeface with ligatures designed around the imprint-code logic, hyper-realistic 3D pill renders deployed at billboard scale, a web layout built off the prescription-bottle label grid, plus contextual OOH (benches, shelters), social activist posts and a national PSA. The system holds across applications and the typography reads from a distance, which is the test for outdoor-led identity work.

impact.rationale: The 8,000+ calls and 142 recovery-story volunteers are the right behaviour-change outcomes for a helpline product and they are absolute counts, which makes them harder to inflate than percentages. The '137 million contacts reached in 4 weeks' is an entrant-reported impressions figure and should be read as a claim rather than an audited number. The press footprint (Bloomberg, AP, Reuters, Forbes, Fast Company, Fox, CBS, ABC) is unusually broad for a niche health intervention and signals genuine earned-media traction.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins Gold because the design turns a mandatory pharmaceutical compliance mark into a working helpline interface — typography becomes a recovery product, not just a poster.

one_liner: A wearable that replaces the white cane with vibration.
search_doc:
A wearable that replaces the white cane with vibration.
Mechanisms: purpose-driven-product, haptic-language-system, inclusive-design-system, industrial-design-craft
Idea: Ara is a wearable self-navigation assistive technology. Using ultrasonic and LiDAR sensors with onboard AI, it scans the environment in real time, identifying potential obstacles and communicating with the body through vibration patterns - a haptic language that is universally understood, breaking down language barriers.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DESIGN/SILVER/Ara - Strap Tech (Bands&People, San Pedro Garza Garcia _ Strap Tech Austin).jpg

extracted.tagline: Self-Navigation and Freedom for the Visually Impaired.
extracted.context: For centuries, people with visual impairment and blindness have used canes as mobility tools. While technology has rapidly evolved, the white cane has not changed at all. No product has solved the major mobility issues for the visually impaired.
extracted.idea: Ara is a wearable self-navigation assistive technology. Using ultrasonic and LiDAR sensors with onboard AI, it scans the environment in real time, identifying potential obstacles and communicating with the body through vibration patterns - a haptic language that is universally understood, breaking down language barriers.
execution: Wearable chest-strap device with ultrasonic and LiDAR sensors
Haptic actuators delivering vibration-based directional cues
Real-time obstacle scanning via onboard AI
Six patents; production-ready product (not a prototype)
Product packaging system and lifestyle photography
Co-created with the community of individuals with different abilities

inferred.one_liner: A wearable that replaces the white cane with vibration.
inferred.qualitative_summary: An industrially designed sensor-and-haptics wearable that cuts collisions by 80% and injury risk by 30% for visually impaired users, awarded by SXSW and Fast Company as world-changing wearable tech.

visual.style_description: Centerpiece is a sculpted matte-black puck-shaped wearable shown in front, side and exploded views; left column carries the problem/solution copy and a results row, right column is a portrait grid showing two users wearing the device on a chest strap plus minimal white packaging.

idea.rationale: Replacing the centuries-old white cane with a chest-worn sensor-and-haptics wearable that speaks a 'vibration language' is a real industrial-design step rather than an awareness object. The reframe of haptic feedback as a universal, language-independent communication layer is the strongest conceptual move.

strategy.rationale: Strap Tech is a young assistive-technology company (Austin / San Pedro Garza Garcia) whose stated mission is mobility for the visually impaired, and Ara is the product the company sells - so the design is mission-fit by definition, not borrowed purpose. The diagnostic of cane stagnation - 'the white cane has not changed at all' while technology around it has - is observable and matches academic literature on assistive mobility, although the board does not cite specific sources. Co-creation with the visually impaired community is explicitly stated.

execution.rationale: Industrial-design craft is the load-bearing layer: a sculpted matte-black puck shown in front, side and exploded views; ultrasonic and LiDAR sensor integration; minimal white packaging system; lifestyle portraiture of two users. Six patents and the explicit 'production-ready, not a prototype' framing matter for credibility. The system coverage is narrower than a full identity rebrand - largely one device, one packaging, one shoot - which limits how far system extensibility can be assessed.

impact.rationale: Headline performance metrics - 80% collision reduction, 30% injury risk reduction and 19% increase in walking speed - are stated as user-outcome figures, but the board does not disclose the test cohort or methodology so '80% of how many users over what period?' remains open. Press logos (Fast Company, Forbes, TIME, SXSW, Expansion, Closing The Gap) are entrant-reported and indicate design-press conversation rather than verified business outcomes. No sales or distribution figure is shown.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the industrial-design craft is genuinely production-grade and the haptic-language concept is original, but the system shown on the board is essentially one device and the performance metrics rest on percentages without a stated test base.

one_liner: The landfill becomes the fashion week.
search_doc:
The landfill becomes the fashion week.
Mechanisms: cultural-symbol-subversion, waste-as-medium, editorial-as-protest, purpose-driven-identity
Idea: Stage a fashion week in the Atacama Desert dressed in the discarded clothes themselves — confronting the industry by mimicking its own language of editorial fashion imagery, in the place where its waste ends up.
Insight: The fashion industry continues to turn its back on the pictures of clothes piling up in the desert; if it presented Atacama as a fashion show, the industry could no longer ignore the textile-waste crisis it created.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DESIGN/SILVER/Atacama Fashion Week - Desierto Vestido & Fashion Revolution (Artplan, São Paulo).webp

extracted.tagline: Look at what the fashion industry refuses to see.
extracted.context: Every year the fashion industry illegally dumps tens of thousands of tonnes of clothing in the Atacama Desert, Chile — turning a natural wonder into a landfill of fast-fashion waste.
extracted.insight: The fashion industry continues to turn its back on the pictures of clothes piling up in the desert; if it presented Atacama as a fashion show, the industry could no longer ignore the textile-waste crisis it created.
extracted.idea: Stage a fashion week in the Atacama Desert dressed in the discarded clothes themselves — confronting the industry by mimicking its own language of editorial fashion imagery, in the place where its waste ends up.
execution: Editorial fashion-week imagery shot on location in the Atacama Desert
Looks composed entirely from clothing dumped in the desert
30 hours of regional and global press coverage
Distribution across earned-media fashion outlets

inferred.one_liner: The landfill becomes the fashion week.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turns the fashion industry's own visual codes back on itself — a fashion week shot in the Atacama landfill earning 30 hours of coverage across major fashion press in 20 countries.

visual.style_description: Wide editorial-fashion photograph of models walking down a 'runway' between mounds of discarded clothing in the Atacama Desert, dressed in salvaged garments; bold orange display type for the title; press logos across the top, dense info columns and a vertical strip of behind-the-scenes shots on the right.

idea.rationale: Restaging the discarded-clothing dumps of the Atacama Desert as an editorial fashion week — same codes, same imagery, same press machine, only the runway is a landfill — is a sharp visual-protest idea that uses the fashion industry's own design language as the weapon against it. The conceptual flip lands instantly on a single image.

strategy.rationale: Fashion Revolution is the international advocacy movement founded after the Rana Plaza collapse to expose the human and environmental cost of fast fashion, and Desierto Vestido is the Chilean collective documenting the Atacama textile dumps, so producing a fashion-week piece from the landfill is core-mission work for both. The cited context — tens of thousands of tonnes of fast-fashion clothing illegally dumped in the Atacama every year — is widely documented by UN Environment Programme and Aljazeera reporting, so the insight has verifiable backing rather than rhetorical force only.

execution.rationale: Execution is essentially a single editorial photoshoot replicated through earned press, with the design craft concentrated in the styling, art direction and location photography rather than a multi-application identity system. By Design-jury standards this reads closer to a PR-led visual stunt than a system: there are no logo, type or applications extending the work, which caps the Execution ceiling even though the photography itself is well-crafted.

impact.rationale: The reported '30 hours of coverage across 20 countries and 20 earned-media outlets' is the right business outcome for an advocacy brief and pickup by Vogue, Elle, The Guardian and Dezeen is high-credibility fashion-press, which would be hard to fake. The numbers are entrant-reported and one of the metrics on the board is truncated ('10 […]'), so the full impact case can't be fully audited. There is no behaviour-change or policy outcome shown — only earned conversation.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the conceptual jiu-jitsu of turning fashion's own editorial language back on its waste is strong, but the work is one well-crafted photoshoot rather than a full design system.

one_liner: Black history printed in ink only black light reveals.
search_doc:
Black history printed in ink only black light reveals.
Mechanisms: invisible-ink-revelation, cultural-heritage-codified, purpose-driven-identity, book-as-artefact, exhibition-design-system
Idea: Design was the crucial element in balancing erasure and history, celebration and protest, darkness and light, in a unique and compelling visual identity. The solution was an intriguing book that had seemingly been erased. An innovative edition entirely printed on special ink that, under a special light - a black light - reveals all its beauty and content. Besides the book, special posters were created that became part of an exhibition.
Insight: Black history has been literally erased from records; revealing it requires a different kind of light.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DESIGN/GOLD/Black Lights -Bradesco (almapBBDO, Sao Paulo).jpg

extracted.tagline: By shedding light on the past, we can illuminate the future.
extracted.context: Brazil has the largest black population outside Africa, yet black people are relegated to erasure: 80% of violent deaths and 70% of school dropouts. A problem dating back to the dawn of humanity violates the constitutional right to memory and robs an entire people of their most precious possession: their history.
extracted.idea: Design was the crucial element in balancing erasure and history, celebration and protest, darkness and light, in a unique and compelling visual identity. The solution was an intriguing book that had seemingly been erased. An innovative edition entirely printed on special ink that, under a special light - a black light - reveals all its beauty and content. Besides the book, special posters were created that became part of an exhibition.
execution: Hardcover book 'Luzes Negras' printed in special UV-reactive ink
Reading and curation by black people
Erased stories revealed under black light - reinforcing missing parts of history
Typography illustrations of black personalities revealed under black light
Posters for the Museu Afro-Brasileiro exhibition
Project supported by Bradesco Seguros in partnership with Project GOMA
metrics: More than 5,800 hours of reading, research, and curatorial work
4 millennia of black history were recovered
440 hours of exhibition time at the Museu Afro in Salvador

inferred.insight: Black history has been literally erased from records; revealing it requires a different kind of light.
inferred.one_liner: Black history printed in ink only black light reveals.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Recovered four millennia of erased black history into a permanent Museu Afro-Brasileiro collection, anchored by a UV-reactive book whose content only appears under black light - turning the act of reading into the act of restoration.

visual.style_description: Hero centered on a black hardcover book whose Portuguese title 'LUZES NEGRAS' glows in pink and blue UV ink; a top strip of case-study film stills shows pages and posters under black light, with side text columns of context and idea, and a metrics ladder on the right.

idea.rationale: Printing a book of erased Black Brazilian history in UV-reactive ink that only appears under a black light is a literal, single-line concept that fuses theme and form. The mechanic - light revealing what was hidden - turns the design artefact itself into the argument, rather than illustrating a message someone else wrote.

strategy.rationale: Bradesco Seguros is one of Brazil's largest insurance groups; partnering with Project GOMA and the Museu Afro-Brasileiro on a cultural-memory artefact extends the brand into protective custodianship of national heritage rather than borrowed purpose. The board cites credible structural insights - Brazil has the largest Black population outside Africa, 80% of violent deaths and 70% of school dropouts - which anchor the cultural-erasure thesis, although those figures are not individually sourced on the board.

execution.rationale: Craft is the load-bearing element: hardcover UV book, typographic illustrations of Black personalities, exhibition posters and Museu Afro installation all sit inside one black-and-fluorescent visual system. The system extends from the printed object to environmental design across 440 hours of exhibition time, holding coherence across applications rather than relying on the book alone.

impact.rationale: Outputs are framed as cultural-heritage figures - 5,800 hours of research and curation and four millennia of Black history recovered - which are the right cultural outcomes for a memory-restoration project, although both are entrant-reported and the 'millennia recovered' framing is rhetorical rather than measurable. The 440 hours of exhibition time at the Museu Afro is a verifiable physical footprint. No business outcome for Bradesco Seguros itself is shown, which is consistent with the cultural-philanthropy framing but limits the brand-impact case.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the UV-reveal mechanic makes the design artefact literally enact its theme - reading the book is the act of restoring history - and the system holds across book, posters and a museum exhibition.

one_liner: The band-aid prints your blood type.
search_doc:
The band-aid prints your blood type.
Mechanisms: everyday-object-transformation, diagnostic-as-product, purpose-driven-product, blood-donation-recruitment
Idea: We developed a new kind of band-aid, which has a thin layer that in seconds when in touch with blood can detect what type of blood that is. Making the information of blood type readily available to everyone who comes for blood exams. This is Blood-Aid.
Insight: Brazilians get blood drawn constantly but leave the lab without ever learning their blood type — the data exists, the patient never sees it.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DESIGN/BRONZE/Blood Aid - Dasa Integrated Health System (DM9, Sao Paulo).jpg

extracted.tagline: A fast, new, cost effective and scalable way to reveal people's blood type.
extracted.context: Blood-Aid was developed to address the fact that 40% of Brazilians don't know their blood type. This lack of knowledge holds Brazilians back from addressing some health precautions that are related to people's blood type as well as to answer to blood-donation calls for specific blood types.
extracted.idea: We developed a new kind of band-aid, which has a thin layer that in seconds when in touch with blood can detect what type of blood that is. Making the information of blood type readily available to everyone who comes for blood exams. This is Blood-Aid.
execution: Engineered band-aid with three layers: microporous adhesive tape, synthetic fiber, and blood-reactive liquid
Applied to the patient's arm after a routine blood draw — type appears as a letter (O−, O+, A−, A+, AB−, AB+, B−, B+)
Distributed across Dasa's diagnostic and integrated health system
Paired with short reply cards inviting wearers to sign up for alerts when their specific blood type is in short supply

inferred.insight: Brazilians get blood drawn constantly but leave the lab without ever learning their blood type — the data exists, the patient never sees it.
inferred.one_liner: The band-aid prints your blood type.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Re-engineers the post-exam band-aid into a personal blood-type reveal and donor-recruitment tool — converting a disposable medical object into a public-health data point and a recurring nudge to donate.

visual.style_description: Editorial product board on a soft beige background with grey speckled border: hero photograph centred on an arm wearing the round Blood-Aid plaster reading 'AB+'; left column text blocks for problem, opportunity and idea; right exploded diagram labels the three layers (microporous adhesive tape, synthetic fiber, blood-reactive liquid); bottom strip lines up product variants for each blood-type letter and four lifestyle shots of the plaster in use.

idea.rationale: Re-engineering the post-exam band-aid into a blood-type reveal product turns a disposable medical object into a personal data point and a donor-recruitment hook. The everyday-object transformation is genuine - using the blood that already gets on the plaster to print the answer the patient never receives.

strategy.rationale: Dasa is Latin America's largest diagnostic medicine network and integrated health system, so closing the loop between a routine blood draw and the patient's knowledge of their own blood type is exact mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The premise that 40% of Brazilians do not know their blood type is the load-bearing context number; it is plausible and matches Brazilian health-system literature on blood-type literacy, but the board does not cite a specific source.

execution.rationale: Product engineering is described with technical clarity - three layers (microporous adhesive, synthetic fibre, blood-reactive liquid), reveal as a single letter, eight blood-type variants, paired reply cards for low-stock donor alerts. The board's design system extends across the product family and lifestyle photography, but the application is essentially one SKU set rather than a wider identity ecosystem, which keeps the design-craft surface narrow.

impact.rationale: The board lists no metrics and no press coverage, so the most natural business outcomes - units distributed across Dasa, downstream donor sign-ups, share of patients newly aware of their blood type - are not quantified. The donor-recruitment loop is described as a mechanism, not as a measured outcome. Without anchored numbers the impact case rests on the product's narrative potential rather than on demonstrated change.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the product idea is mission-fit and surgically simple, but the design canvas is essentially one SKU set and the board carries no metrics or press to anchor real-world impact.

campaign: Days of Hirayama — Perfect Days official site
one_liner: The film becomes a scrollable book.
search_doc:
The film becomes a scrollable book.
Mechanisms: scroll-as-narrative, kinetic-typography, film-extension-as-design, digital-craft-as-the-point
Idea: This website aimed to create a new form of expression that cannot be achieved through the medium of film. After thorough examination of how the film came to life, what it aimed to portray, and of the film's cultural values, it became clear that the best way of expressing all of these components on a website platform was to create a 'scroll book'.
Insight: A film about quiet daily routines cannot be re-told as a trailer; it needs a slow, tactile reading rhythm that the cinema can't offer.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DESIGN/SILVER/Days Of Hirayama -  Perfect Days (Dentsu Tokyo).jpg

extracted.campaign: Days of Hirayama — Perfect Days official site
extracted.tagline: A scrollable book created by reassembling a movie's sense of time and storyline.
extracted.context: The official website for Wim Wenders' most recent film PERFECT DAYS — gaining worldwide attention after receiving the Best Actor Award at the 76th Cannes International Film Festival and nominated for Best International Feature at the 96th Academy Awards — takes a different approach in expressing what the film tries to portray. It explores the various forms of expression that can only be realized on a digital platform.
extracted.idea: This website aimed to create a new form of expression that cannot be achieved through the medium of film. After thorough examination of how the film came to life, what it aimed to portray, and of the film's cultural values, it became clear that the best way of expressing all of these components on a website platform was to create a 'scroll book'.
execution: Scroll-driven digital book: type sways, blurs and forms images as the user scrolls
Touch / cursor movement triggers sound and motion responses
353 days of Hirayama's daily life written specifically for the site, beyond the 12 shown in the film
Optimised for both smartphone and laptop reading
Linked from the film's official website (www.perfectdays-movie.jp, QR code)
metrics: 750 million JPY box office in Japan (film, end of January 2024)
press_coverage: The interactivity drew me into the world of the movie even more than the movie did. — @hktchr_
I read all the way to the end, captivated by the prose, sound, and design. — @takahirolling
Through words and sound alone, you can vividly picture the way the protagonist moves and lives. — @camimu

inferred.insight: A film about quiet daily routines cannot be re-told as a trailer; it needs a slow, tactile reading rhythm that the cinema can't offer.
inferred.one_liner: The film becomes a scrollable book.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Re-renders a Wim Wenders film as a scroll-driven typographic book extending Hirayama's routine to 353 days; drew 810,000 views and 280,000 users with viral fan testimony, accompanying a film that grossed 750M JPY in Japan.

visual.style_description: Dark cinematic board: left half holds large white serif title 'Days of HIRAYAMA' over text columns of background, idea, strategy, execution and result; right half is a moody black scroll-typography hero where letterforms blur and dissolve into motion-blurred Japanese characters; bottom strip shows seven thumbnails of the website's scroll states from film stills to handwritten pages.

idea.rationale: Re-rendering a Wim Wenders film about quiet daily routine as a scroll-driven typographic book, where letterforms sway, blur and dissolve in time with the user's gesture, is a precise piece of digital-craft thinking. The reframe treats the website as something the cinema cannot do - a slow, tactile reading rhythm - rather than a marketing site for a movie.

strategy.rationale: Perfect Days is Wim Wenders' Cannes Best Actor and Academy-nominated 2023 feature, and the site is the film's official Japanese vehicle - so the design's mission is to extend the film's authorial voice, not invent a new one. Coherence between the film's meditative subject matter and a slow scroll-book is direct rather than borrowed. The 353 days of Hirayama's life written specifically for the site (vs the 12 days shown in the film) is a real authorial extension.

execution.rationale: Kinetic-typography craft is the load-bearing element: scroll-triggered sway, blur and image formation, sound responses to cursor movement, and a responsive system across smartphone and laptop reading. Distribution is anchored through QR linkage from the film's official site. The system is narrower than a multi-application identity - effectively one digital surface - but the surface is where the craft is exhibited.

impact.rationale: 810,000 views and 280,000 users are anchored absolutes, the right surface metrics for a digital design artefact, although neither is independently audited and there is no benchmark comparison. The 750M JPY Japan box office is a film-business figure that this design extension cannot directly claim credit for. The fan testimony quotes - 'the interactivity drew me into the world of the movie even more than the movie did' - are entrant-curated but credible signals of cultural resonance.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the scroll-book turns kinetic typography into the film's emotional rhythm, but the work lives on a single digital surface and the impact case mixes the site's own metrics with the film's box office.

one_liner: A confection becomes the next page of the novel.
search_doc:
A confection becomes the next page of the novel.
Mechanisms: packaging-as-ritual, cultural-heritage-codified, purpose-driven-product, multi-sensory-design
Idea: To preserve the traditions of wagashi and provide a deeper literary experience for Japanese classic novels, a Kyoto-based wagashi craftsman partnered with a publisher of Japan's most iconic authors to create Bungashi — bespoke wagashi designed and crafted to be enjoyed during specific sections of the novel. Packaged together, the reader is notified within the pages of the book to add the wagashi to the story.
Insight: Wagashi already encodes seasonal and emotional symbolism — pairing each sweet with a chapter turns reading itself into a multi-sensorial cultural ritual.

extracted.tagline: Adding a multi-sensorial experience to reading a classic Japanese novel.
extracted.context: Wagashi is the traditional art of Japanese confectionery. Going back centuries, it reflects the essence and beauty of the natural world. But in recent years, today's confections have lost their cultural connection, and their true beauty.
extracted.idea: To preserve the traditions of wagashi and provide a deeper literary experience for Japanese classic novels, a Kyoto-based wagashi craftsman partnered with a publisher of Japan's most iconic authors to create Bungashi — bespoke wagashi designed and crafted to be enjoyed during specific sections of the novel. Packaged together, the reader is notified within the pages of the book to add the wagashi to the story.
execution: Bespoke wagashi sweets designed to match specific novel scenes
Hardcover book with illustrated cover, packaged with wagashi set
In-book notifications pairing each sweet to a chapter or passage
Limited edition retail product

inferred.insight: Wagashi already encodes seasonal and emotional symbolism — pairing each sweet with a chapter turns reading itself into a multi-sensorial cultural ritual.
inferred.one_liner: A confection becomes the next page of the novel.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Pairing centuries-old wagashi craft with classic Japanese literature created a multi-sensorial reading product that lifted sales 280% and reached 97% positive sentiment.

visual.style_description: Minimalist still-life composition with the hardcover novel standing alongside artisan wagashi sweets on a clean off-white surface; small black Bungashi logo and centred sans-serif title; body copy laid out in three even columns underneath; bottom strip shows process and tasting photography plus press logos.

idea.rationale: Pairing handcrafted wagashi sweets with specific chapters of classic Japanese novels — the reader is prompted by the page to taste a confection designed for that scene — is a charming multi-sensory product idea. The concept is elegant but sits closer to a premium-product collaboration than a category-defining design reframe.

strategy.rationale: Kameya Yoshinaga is a Kyoto-based wagashi craftsman (the brand traces its history to traditional Kyoto confectionery houses) whose business depends on keeping wagashi culturally relevant against industrial confectionery, so co-publishing edible literature with a Japanese-classics publisher is direct heritage-preservation mission-fit. The cited insight — that wagashi has lost its cultural connection to seasonal and literary symbolism in recent decades — is consistent with documented declines in traditional confectionery consumption in Japan, but the board doesn't cite a specific source.

execution.rationale: The craft is concentrated in the wagashi themselves and the hardcover packaging, and the in-book pairing system is the right mechanism for the concept. By Design-Lions standards the application surface is narrow: a single limited-edition retail product with packaging, a book cover and process photography — there is no extended identity, typographic or environmental system, which holds Execution in the upper-Bronze band even though the individual artefacts are well-made.

impact.rationale: The '+280% sales' is unanchored — 280% of what baseline volume for a limited-edition Kyoto wagashi product? — and is best read as a directional signal rather than a hard business outcome. The 97% positive sentiment is consistent with a niche premium-product launch but the sample base isn't disclosed. The press footprint (JIJI, Yahoo News Japan, Fashion Press, Sankei) is credible Japanese mainstream coverage and points to genuine earned attention.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the wagashi-meets-literature pairing is a charming heritage-preservation product, but the work is a single limited-edition SKU rather than a design system and both headline metrics are unanchored percentages.

extracted.tagline: The most ludicrously targeted campaign in history
extracted.context: Only 16.2% of people in the UK have visited a museum in the last 12 months. Many believe they don't have the time. But the V&A museum has over 2.8 million items: it's the most wide-ranging collection in the UK.
extracted.insight: The campaign harnesses the breadth of the V&A collection to prove the truth of our proposition and pay tribute to the nation's obsessions in the process.
extracted.idea: Hundreds of hyper-targeted posters and digital placements, each pairing a niche interest (everything from cosplay to skate culture) with a specific artefact from the V&A's 2.8 million-object collection that matches it — proving the museum has something for everyone.
execution: Hundreds of unique poster executions
OOH placements on London Underground, bus stops, street furniture
Tote bags and merchandise
Press, digital and social ads
Hyper-local targeting matching audience interest to museum object

inferred.one_liner: Whatever you're into, the V&A already has it.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A system of hundreds of niche-specific posters turns the V&A's 2.8M object archive into a personalised invitation, driving a 12,000% spike in collection searches and 37M impressions.

visual.style_description: Dense modular grid of dozens of small bright-yellow and red poster tiles, each pairing a single object photograph with a short headline; black background ties the system together; campaign title in big yellow type top-left; metric row and press logos along the bottom strip.

idea.rationale: Hundreds of hyper-niche posters, each pairing a single subculture interest with a specific object from the V&A's 2.8 million-piece collection, is a smart art-direction idea that uses scale itself as the concept — the breadth of the collection becomes the breadth of the campaign. It is a system idea more than a single-asset idea, which is on-brief for Design Lions.

strategy.rationale: The V&A is the UK's national museum of art, design and performance, with a public-service mission of broadening cultural access, so a campaign built on proving the collection covers every niche interest is direct mission-fit. The cited insight — only 16.2% of UK adults visited a museum in the last 12 months — is a DCMS-style participation figure that is verifiable, and the 2.8 million-object catalogue size is publicly documented. The strategic frame is solid even if the precise sourcing isn't on the board.

execution.rationale: The craft test for this idea is whether the system holds across hundreds of executions — and the board shows the modular grid of posters reading coherently on a tube wall, on bus stops, in press and on tote-bag merchandise. The art direction is competent rather than category-defining: the layout language (red/yellow tile + object + headline) is consistent but visually flatter than the Gold-tier identity systems it sits next to in this jury pool, which holds the Execution score in the high-Silver band.

impact.rationale: The 23% increase in V&A searches is a credible directional outcome for an awareness brief, and the 37 million campaign impressions is an entrant-reported reach claim that should be read as a media-buy figure rather than verified behaviour. The headline '+12,000% rise in collection searches' is dramatic but unanchored — 12,000% of what baseline, and over what window? Press pickup from BBC, CNN, TIME and Rolling Stone is high-credibility earned support.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the niche-poster system genuinely showcases the V&A's range, but the art direction sits below the identity-system craft of the Gold winners and the impact rests on one unanchored percentage.

inferred.one_liner: The beer crate becomes the neighbourhood furniture.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Redesigns Colombia's iconic Poker yellow crate into a modular furniture system that converts corner stores into social hubs, lifting sales by reported double-digit percentages across participating outlets.

visual.style_description: Hero is a warm, lit shot of three friends sitting around a yellow Poker-crate table at a neighbourhood store, with crates re-stacked as a foosball table and chairs; bottom strip catalogues the eight modular furniture configurations and a small results row carries three percentage gains.

review_reasons: small image resolution; some metric units and context numbers partially unreadable

idea.rationale: Redesigning the iconic yellow 'petaco' beer crate so its modules snap into chairs, tables, dominoes boards and foosball is a deft act of everyday-object transformation. The crate already functions as informal furniture in Colombian neighbourhood stores - the idea formalises that latent use into a system rather than inventing a new artefact.

strategy.rationale: Poker Beer is Colombia's dominant national lager (AB-InBev / Bavaria portfolio), and its 'tienda de barrio' channel - the neighbourhood store - is the brand's heartland sales surface. Turning the brand's existing logistics packaging into the social furniture of that exact channel is direct mission-fit, anchored in genuine Colombian drinking culture rather than borrowed purpose. The board's context paragraph references in-store sales dynamics, although key numbers are partially unreadable on the asset.

execution.rationale: The yellow Poker colour is held as the system signature across eight modular configurations, with consistent product photography and in-store activation imagery. Craft is in the snap-fit module logic - one crate, many objects - rather than in surface graphics. System extensibility is real; the limit is that the work shown is primarily product photography and one staged store activation rather than a wider identity ecosystem.

impact.rationale: The board cites three percentage gains - +30%, +46% and +75% - but the unit labels and bases are partially illegible or absent ('[stores]', '[with the same budget]', '[...]'), so each figure is a soft claim rather than a verifiable business outcome. Beer-volume or revenue uplift, the natural business outcome for an in-trade activation, is not anchored to an absolute base on the board. No press coverage is recorded.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the crate-as-furniture system formalises a real Colombian neighbourhood ritual into a coherent product family, but the impact case is built on percentages whose bases the board does not fully disclose.

one_liner: The smartwatch becomes a speech therapist on your wrist.
search_doc:
The smartwatch becomes a speech therapist on your wrist.
Mechanisms: wearable-as-therapy-device, haptic-as-instruction, ai-powered-accessibility, purpose-driven-product
Idea: A free A.I. app for Galaxy Watch that transforms words into haptic vibrations to help people with speech disorders speak fluently, using rhythmic vibrations on the wrist to guide fluent speech.
Insight: A democratization of speech therapy, making it universally accessible for all.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DESIGN/GOLD/Impulse - Samsung (Cheil Madrid).jpg

extracted.tagline: The first A.I. powered haptic speech therapist for speaking disorders.
extracted.context: Speech disorders such as stuttering, dysarthria and ALS are widespread; existing therapy is hard to access, leaving people without consistent support.
extracted.insight: A democratization of speech therapy, making it universally accessible for all.
extracted.idea: A free A.I. app for Galaxy Watch that transforms words into haptic vibrations to help people with speech disorders speak fluently, using rhythmic vibrations on the wrist to guide fluent speech.
execution: Free Galaxy Watch app that converts words into haptic vibrations
Natural Language Processing engine translates syllables into rhythmic vibrations
Uses the proven rhythm-therapy technique of speech therapists
Designed to improve expression and tone of voice
Built for public speaking — vibrations suit any stage anywhere, anytime
metrics: Endorsed by the Spanish and Portuguese speech associations
Developed in partnership with +15 speech therapists and medical professionals from Spain, Portugal, USA, UAE & Ghana

inferred.one_liner: The smartwatch becomes a speech therapist on your wrist.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Repositions the Galaxy Watch as an accessible speech-therapy tool, validated by speech associations in Spain and Portugal and tested with patients over six months.

visual.style_description: Hero close-up photograph of a wrist wearing a Galaxy Watch with a colourful audio-wave dial, set against a dark blurred concert-crowd background; left column holds the Impulse wordmark and dense body copy, right column lists results and clinician portraits in clean grid.

idea.rationale: Translating words into wrist-borne haptic rhythms so people with speech disorders can synchronise their cadence to the watch's beat is a strong product-design idea — a category-native repurposing of the smartwatch's haptic engine as a therapy instrument. The conceptual leap from 'wearable as notification device' to 'wearable as speech-therapy metronome' is original and on-brief for an accessibility-positioned Galaxy Watch.

strategy.rationale: Samsung is the global consumer-electronics brand whose Galaxy ecosystem is positioned around mainstream accessibility ('Together for Tomorrow'), so building a free clinical-grade therapy app on the Galaxy Watch sits squarely inside the brand mission rather than borrowing purpose. The clinical premise — that rhythmic pacing techniques are an established speech-therapy method — is real and is reinforced by partnership with 15+ speech therapists across Spain, Portugal, USA, UAE and Ghana plus endorsement from the Spanish and Portuguese speech associations, which gives the insight verifiable backing.

execution.rationale: Execution is product-craft rather than identity-craft: a free Galaxy Watch app with an NLP engine that converts syllables into rhythmic vibrations, designed to work on stage or in everyday speech, tested with patients for 6+ months. The board reads more as a product launch than a design-system showcase — there is limited demonstration of how the visual language (wave-form dial, identity grid) extends across applications, which is the typical Design-jury benchmark for an 80+ Execution score.

impact.rationale: The clinical validation signals are the strongest part of the case: 6+ months of patient testing, endorsement from the Spanish and Portuguese speech associations, and a 4.5/5 user rating that — while the absolute sample size isn't disclosed — is consistent with a credible clinical pilot. There are no business outcomes (downloads, active users, adoption by clinicians) on the board, so the impact rests on expert validation and qualitative usability rather than scale evidence.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins Gold because it repositions a consumer smartwatch as a clinically-validated speech-therapy device, with real expert endorsement standing in for scale metrics.

one_liner: The bottle stays locked until its time is up.

The bottle stays locked until its time is up.

Idea: Build the Barrique limited-edition wine into a box with an integrated padlock that opens it only at a pre-programmed moment, making the wine mature over time and turning the act of waiting into the experience itself.

inferred.one_liner: The bottle stays locked until its time is up.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turns a premium wine release into a deferred-gratification ritual: a padlocked box paired with an app that opens only at the pre-set moment, dramatising the role of time in a wine's life.

visual.style_description: Hero shot of a tall black Barrique presentation box with embossed gold typography, set against a deep black backdrop; a grid of nine product and app-UI photographs runs across the right and bottom — bottle, opened box revealing the gold-lined interior, and a sequence of black-on-black phone screens showing the timer steps.

idea.rationale: Padlocking a wine bottle inside a box that opens only at a pre-programmed moment turns the act of waiting into the product experience. It is a clear packaging-as-ritual move - the box stages the wine's aging-time argument in a single mechanic - though time-lock packaging exists in luxury spirits and the move is more refinement than category-defining.

strategy.rationale: Franc Arman is a small Istrian premium wine producer (Croatia), and Barrique is its limited-edition red. The insight that consumers open red wines before they reach peak quality is a credible category truth in fine-wine education, even though the board does not cite specific consumer research. Coherence between a premium positioning and a ceremonial time-locked unveiling is direct mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose.

execution.rationale: The black-and-gold presentation box, embossed typography, QR-linked companion app and countdown UI sit inside a consistent premium identity. Craft on the physical object reads as polished luxury packaging; the app screens extend the system into a digital surface, which is a real extension beyond a one-off SKU. System reach is still narrow - one box, one app, one bottle - and the engineering of the padlock mechanism is not shown in detail.

impact.rationale: The board carries no metrics and no press coverage on the extraction, so sell-through, brand-search lift or design-press recognition cannot be assessed from what is shown. The natural business outcome for a premium wine identity refresh - distribution gains or price-tier movement - is not anchored. Cultural footprint inside the wine-design community is plausible but unverified on the asset.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the time-lock box dramatises the wine's aging-time argument with confident premium craft, but the design system is narrow and the board offers no metrics or press to anchor cultural or business impact.

inferred.qualitative_summary: Stands up a circular sports-merchandise brand whose collect-repurpose-redistribute loop turned Inter Miami waste into a second collection presented at Art Basel Miami and covered by BoF, Versus and SoccerBible.

visual.style_description: Dark editorial sports-fashion board: hero centre is a model wearing a reconstructed jersey on a hot-pink torn-fabric backdrop with a faint (Re)boot circular symbol behind; left column states the three-action system with icons; right column tiles editorial product shots, athlete portraits and lookbook frames; bottom strip credits collaborators (BoF, MLS Official Anti-Waste Collection, Carlos Alberto, Inter Miami Official Brand).

idea.rationale: Building an anti-waste brand around a three-step Collect–Repurpose–Redistribute loop for pro-sports kit is a coherent circular-merchandise idea, and the parenthesised '(Re)boot' wordmark encodes that loop into the logo itself. The conceptual frame is sound but circular-fashion brands in this exact register are now a crowded design space, which holds the originality ceiling.

strategy.rationale: (Re)boot is a newly-built anti-waste brand whose declared mission is to circularise pro-sports kit consumption, and its first collaboration is with Inter Miami CF (an MLS club whose merchandise machine is a credible source of the waste being addressed) — so the brand-to-mission fit is clean by construction. The cited context (clubs generating large volumes of discarded uniforms while marketing new collections each season) is broadly verifiable through sports-merchandise industry reporting, but the board does not quote specific waste tonnage figures.

execution.rationale: Execution covers a brand identity (wordmark, three-action circular symbol, pink signature colour), an apparel collection rebuilt from reclaimed jerseys, editorial product photography, the Art Basel Miami showcase and a Carlos Alberto capsule collab. The identity system is competent and extensible, but the typographic and graphic craft sits below the Gold/Silver benchmarks in this jury pool — the pink-and-black sports-fashion register is on-trend rather than category-defining.

impact.rationale: The board carries no metrics: no units produced, units sold, revenue, waste-tonnes diverted or social engagement numbers are shown. Press footprint is narrow (Business of Fashion, Versus, SoccerBible) — credible niche fashion/football coverage but limited mainstream pickup. For a circular-fashion brief whose entire purpose is to demonstrate measurable circularity, the absence of any quantified waste-diversion outcome is a real soft spot.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the brand identity and Inter Miami collaboration are competently built, but the design system is on-trend rather than original and the board carries no quantified circularity outcome to anchor the case.

one_liner: Bring back the watch by bringing back its decade.
search_doc:
Bring back the watch by bringing back its decade.
Mechanisms: design-era-revival, brand-platform-extension, editorial-design-system, craft-as-the-point
Idea: We not only brought back the watch from the 70s but the entire decade. Also, just like the new IWC Ingenieur, in a modern interpretation. A retro-futuristic parallel world of brand communication inspired by the most influential designers of the 70s.
Insight: If a 1970s icon is being reissued, the campaign world around it should be reissued too - in the exact design vocabulary of its decade.

inferred.insight: If a 1970s icon is being reissued, the campaign world around it should be reissued too - in the exact design vocabulary of its decade.
inferred.one_liner: Bring back the watch by bringing back its decade.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reintroduces the Gérald Genta-designed IWC Ingenieur with a 70s-inspired retro-futurist design system extending across editorial, posters and social - earning The New York Times coverage and reframing a product launch as a decade revival.

visual.style_description: Hero is a hyper-detailed silver IWC Ingenieur watch on a black studio background paired with a portrait of Gérald Genta and a NYT quote; surrounding it are tiled mock-ups of magazine spreads, posters and social posts in a 70s-inspired vintage palette of warm browns, oranges and metallic silver.

idea.rationale: Reintroducing the 1970s Gerald Genta-designed IWC Ingenieur by rebuilding the brand-communication world in the exact design vocabulary of the 70s is a coherent move, but design-era revival is a well-trodden pattern in luxury launches. The concept is on-brief and well-executed rather than category-defining.

strategy.rationale: IWC Schaffhausen is the Swiss Richemont-group haute horlogerie brand whose 'Form und Technik' positioning has anchored the Ingenieur line since the original Genta design. Coupling the reissue of a 1970s icon with a 1970s-language campaign is direct mission-fit - the brand is referencing its own design heritage rather than borrowing from outside. The board cites a 40-year demand from collectors as the trigger but does not back the figure with sales or community-survey data.

execution.rationale: The retro-futurist design system extends across magazine spreads, posters, social posts and editorial storytelling around Genta, holding a consistent 70s typographic and palette signature. Application breadth is real, but the surface is largely editorial and advertising layouts rather than a deeper Design Lions canon (no signage, no packaging redesign, no UI system); craft is competent and on-period without breaking new ground in typography or composition.

impact.rationale: The board carries no quantified metrics - no sales lift, brand-search uplift, social-conversation figure or wait-list data. The New York Times coverage is an entrant-reported press claim and a credible directional signal of editorial pickup, but it cannot carry the impact case alone for a luxury watch launch where the natural business outcome would be sell-through or pre-order demand.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the decade-revival design system is on-brand and well-crafted but covers familiar luxury-launch territory, and the impact case rests on a single press claim with no quantified business outcome.

idea.rationale: Generating the visual identity directly from real audio recordings of the ecosystems the organisation defends — every letter shape, numeral and graphic element born from a literal sound waveform — is an elegant data-to-design translation. The conceptual one-liner ('the planet's own voice becomes the brand') is a strong identity-system idea native to Design.

strategy.rationale: Planetary Guardians is an advocacy collective of scientists, politicians and activists organised around the Stockholm Resilience Centre's planetary-boundaries framework, so an identity that lets the planet 'speak' through generative shapes is precise mission-fit rather than borrowed nature symbolism. The board does not cite the planetary-boundaries science source explicitly, but the underlying framework (Rockström et al.) is well-documented and the source ecosystems named (Barrier Reef, Arctic Tern, Mangrove Forest, Atacama, Coral Atolls) are real.

execution.rationale: The craft is genuinely systemic: a generative wordmark, a bespoke alphabet and numeral set, ecosystem 'signatures' for at least eight different biomes, and a modular application grid spanning reports, posters, type specimens and web. The sound-to-shape rendering reads cleanly at logo scale and the system is visibly extensible. The application surface is somewhat narrower than the Gold benchmarks — the work shown is mostly editorial and digital rather than environmental or product-led.

impact.rationale: The board carries no metrics, no press logos and no behaviour-change or fundraising evidence — impact rests entirely on the craft of the identity and the assumption that a Planetary Guardians coalition is itself a high-status platform. For a brand-identity refresh, qualitative impact (design-press pickup, peer recognition) can substitute for hard numbers, but here neither is shown on the board.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver on the strength of the data-to-identity craft alone — the generative system is genuinely original, but with no metrics or press evidence on the board the impact case is conspicuously thin.

Cultural footprint inside the creative-tech community is plausible but unverified on the board.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the site genuinely IS the demo - a piece of real-time 3D craft that proves the capability it sells - but with no metrics or press claims on the board the impact case rests entirely on craft reputation.

Burning paper organs restores what the dead gave away.
Mechanisms: ritual-creation, cultural-tradition-integration, purpose-driven-product, craft-as-the-point
Idea: Paper Organs is an innovative product following a common funeral custom in Taiwan — 'Joss Paper Burning'. Families burn paper replicas of money, houses and clothes so the deceased receive them in the afterlife. Paper Organs are crafted to be burned in temples during rituals, restoring what was donated and reassuring families that the body remains intact, aligned with traditional Chinese custom.
Insight: If grieving families could symbolically 'return' donated organs to the deceased via joss-paper ritual, the cultural taboo against organ donation could be neutralised.

inferred.one_liner: Burning paper organs restores what the dead gave away.
inferred.qualitative_summary: By marrying organ donation to Taiwan's joss-paper burning ritual, the product reframes a centuries-old taboo and lifts positive donation discussion by 411% in six months.

visual.style_description: Cinematic hero shot of a glowing paper-craft anatomical heart on fire against a black background with embers, framed by short context and idea text blocks at top; bottom strip shows four other paper organ artefacts (kidneys, lungs, etc.) and a metric row leading into hospital partner logos.

idea.rationale: Designing organ-shaped joss paper so families can symbolically 'return' a donated heart, kidney or cornea to the deceased through the existing burning ritual is a precise ritual-design idea: it does not argue against the centuries-old 'body must remain intact' taboo, it slots a new artefact into the cultural practice that enforces it. A genuinely original product-design solution to a behavioural problem.

strategy.rationale: The Taiwan Organ Sharing Registry and Patient Autonomy Promotion Center is the national NGO tasked with promoting organ donation in Taiwan, so creating a ritual artefact aimed at the religious taboo blocking donor consent is mission-defining work rather than borrowed purpose. The insight — that traditional Taoist/Buddhist belief in bodily integrity at death is the dominant cultural barrier to donor registration — is widely documented in Taiwanese public-health literature, and the joss-paper-burning practice is a real, daily ritual the artefact slots into.

execution.rationale: The craft is in the artefacts themselves — paper-folded hearts, kidneys, corneas, lungs and livers made in the traditional joss-paper style — and they are distributed through the right channels (partner hospitals and temples) where the ritual actually happens. The system is narrower than a multi-application identity — there is no extended typographic or graphic system shown — but for a ritual-product brief the artefact IS the design, and the craft of the paper folding is high. The application surface (332 sets across five organ types in six months) is modest in scale.

impact.rationale: The 3,400+ donor consent forms signed is the right behaviour-change outcome for an organ-donation brief and is an absolute number tied to a regulated, verifiable registry. 76% of family members finding comfort is a credible qualitative measure though the sample base isn't disclosed on the board. The '+411% positive discussion volume' is unanchored — 411% of what baseline social volume? — and is best read as directional. Recruitment of 11+ executing hospitals into the program is a tangible institutional outcome.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the artefact genuinely engineers a ritual workaround to a centuries-old taboo and produces measurable consent forms — held below Gold by the modest distribution scale (332 sets) and one unanchored percentage.

one_liner: One thread pulled from the Pride flag for every hate incident.
search_doc:
One thread pulled from the Pride flag for every hate incident.
Mechanisms: data-as-physical-artefact, cultural-symbol-subversion, purpose-driven-identity, exhibition-as-advocacy
Idea: We tracked every Canadian anti-LGBTQ+ protest and scrubbed every Canadian social post for hate speech used in conjunction with LGBTQ+ terms. This uncovered 6,423 hate-fuelled incidents from just the first three months of 2023. We took the Progress Pride flag, the symbol of Pride itself, and removed 6,423 threads — one thread for each act of hate against the LGBTQ+ community in Canada. The unravelled flag appeared in a campaign that directed Canadians to spread the message and stand up against hate. It was also used to lobby the Canadian government to take urgent action.
Insight: An invisible statistic becomes a visceral image when the symbol it harms - the Pride flag - is literally unravelled thread by thread.

idea.rationale: Removing one thread from the Progress Pride flag for each of the 6,423 anti-LGBTQ+ hate incidents recorded in three months turns the symbol of Pride itself into a counted dataset. It is a precise data-as-physical-artefact move: the icon does not represent the data, it IS the data.

strategy.rationale: Egale Canada is Canada's largest 2SLGBTQI advocacy organisation, so visualising anti-LGBTQ+ hate in Canada is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The premise is anchored in a verifiable institutional fact - the Canadian government stopped tracking anti-LGBTQ+ hate in 2021 - which makes the data-vacuum diagnostic credible. The 6,423 figure is sourced from Egale's own tracking of protests and social posts, which is entrant-reported but methodologically explicit.

execution.rationale: The unravelled-flag artefact is the design system, extended into a physical exhibition piece at Pride events, into print and OOH calling people to act, and into influencer-distributed flag content. Craft holds on the hero artefact, but the supporting applications are largely advertising executions rather than a full design-system canon - the work sits at the edge between data-visualisation craft and a campaign asset.

impact.rationale: The strongest outcome is policy: $1.5M in Canadian government funding for free event security and the federal government's first report on anti-LGBTQ+ hate in over two years - both are real institutional changes, not surface metrics. The 73M earned impressions are entrant-reported and unaudited, and 'six out of ten people who recalled the campaign would donate' is a recall-stated intent rather than a banked donation. The press logo wall (CBC, CTV, Toronto Star and others) is a credible breadth signal of earned conversation.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the unravelled flag is a precise data-as-artefact move that drove a real Canadian government response, but the supporting design system tips into advocacy-advertising rather than a multi-application craft canon.

visual.style_description: Hero overhead shot of a textured grey concrete sidewalk with a white cane mid-stride across the new tactile patterns; right side shows a vertical column of small cement tiles, each engraved with a category icon and label (Restaurant, Bank, Grocery Store, etc.); body copy in three columns at the left edge, metric figures along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Extending the existing universal tactile-paving vocabulary with two new patterns - a horizontal access bar and a barcode-style category icon - reframes the sidewalk itself as a readable map for blind users. It is a category-defining design move that turns urban infrastructure into a public interface rather than producing another awareness asset.

strategy.rationale: Sol Cement is Peru's leading cement brand, so building the brand's product material - concrete tiles - into the public good is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The premise that current tactile paving tells the blind where to walk but not where they are is an observable cultural truth, even though the board does not back it with a stat. Releasing the system open-source positions it as long-term infrastructure rather than a one-off campaign.

execution.rationale: The craft holds at every scale: a coherent icon set across ten venue categories (Restaurant, Bank, Grocery Store, Drugstore, Bus Stop, Hospital, Hair Salon, Supermarket, Mobile Store, Lodging), modular tile production, and city-scale deployment across the Miraflores district in partnership with Peru's largest associations for the visually impaired. System extensibility is built in by design - it is open-source, so any city can adopt the same vocabulary.

impact.rationale: Headline numbers are anchored in absolutes - 75,000 m of implemented sidewalk and 500,000 people benefited - which is the right business-outcome framing for a public-infrastructure design project. The 'benefited' figure is entrant-reported rather than independently audited, but the deployed surface area is a verifiable physical artefact. The open-source release adds durable cultural footprint beyond the launch window.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because it extends a universal accessibility standard into a city-scale, open-source design system - turning a cement brand's raw material into the readable map of Lima itself.

A Holocaust survivor reads the paper among the stelae.

Idea: For the 100th edition, the campaign turned the format into an anti-Semitism statement: the cover places the FAZ in the hands of Holocaust survivor Margot Friedländer at the Memorial to the Murdered Jews of Europe in Berlin, releasing the cover on the anniversary of the Discovery of Auschwitz.
Insight: The FAZ's signature 'Brilliant Minds' cover device — defining a person not by their face but by where they stand — could carry uniquely heavy meaning when the person is a Holocaust survivor standing among the stelae of the Berlin Memorial.

For decades it has been famous for its 'Brilliant Minds' series — covers featuring intellectual portraits hidden in the newspaper, with only their surroundings to reveal their identities. With around 90 editions in 29 years, the campaign has featured Oscar winners, Nobel laureates, sports legends, German chancellors and many more.

execution: Photographic cover of Margot Friedländer reading the FAZ at the Berlin Holocaust Memorial
Released on the 79th anniversary of the discovery of Auschwitz
Distributed as part of the 'Brilliant Minds' series
Triggered a national debate on contemporary anti-Semitism
press_coverage: "A photo that moves you to the core…"
"An exceptional image from an exceptional campaign" — dvgD

inferred.insight: The FAZ's signature 'Brilliant Minds' cover device — defining a person not by their face but by where they stand — could carry uniquely heavy meaning when the person is a Holocaust survivor standing among the stelae of the Berlin Memorial.
inferred.one_liner: A Holocaust survivor reads the paper among the stelae.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Repurposes a long-running newspaper cover device into a national statement against anti-Semitism, generating wide German press pickup and editorial endorsement on the anniversary of the discovery of Auschwitz.

visual.style_description: Editorial cover layout: a dramatic top-down photograph of the Berlin Memorial to the Murdered Jews of Europe with Margot Friedländer reading the FAZ at the centre of the grey stelae; left column carries the FAZ wordmark, body copy and a small portrait of Friedländer; bottom strip carries four press quote pull-outs and a row of media logos.

idea.rationale: Using the FAZ's 29-year-old 'Brilliant Minds' format — defining a person by where they stand rather than by their face — to place Holocaust survivor Margot Friedländer among the stelae of the Berlin Memorial on the anniversary of the discovery of Auschwitz is a precise, format-native editorial idea. The conceptual weight comes from the constraint, not from a new device.

strategy.rationale: The Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung is one of Germany's two papers of record and its 'Brilliant Minds' series is a decades-long brand institution — using its 100th edition to make a statement on contemporary anti-Semitism is squarely on-mission for a German newspaper of record at a moment of rising domestic anti-Semitism. The anniversary of the discovery of Auschwitz is the most loaded possible date for the message in Germany, and Margot Friedländer is a verifiable, publicly recognised Holocaust witness — the strategic frame is precise and culturally calibrated.

execution.rationale: Execution is a single editorial cover photograph plus its distribution as part of the running series. The craft is in the photography itself — the top-down composition placing Friedländer at the centre of the stelae grid, the discipline of leaving her face partly hidden in line with the series format — but there is no extended identity or application system beyond the cover and the resulting press conversation. By Design-Lions standards this is a single-asset piece, which limits Execution ceiling.

impact.rationale: Impact rests on triggering 'a national debate on contemporary anti-Semitism' and on press endorsement — ARD, Süddeutsche, Die Welt, Horizont, dvgD all carry quotes endorsing the piece. There are no audited reach, circulation-lift or subscription numbers on the board, and several of the press quotes contain unjustified superlatives ('exceptional image from an exceptional campaign') that are themselves the kind of vague praise RIGOUR flags. The cultural significance is real but the impact case is qualitative rather than quantified.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the format-recontextualisation is editorially precise and the moment well-chosen, but the work is one cover photograph rather than a design system and the impact is press endorsement rather than measured outcome.

Idea: As families reunited after a three-year hiatus, IGA gave Quebec's people a hardcover holiday gift to protect their precious culinary heritage forever: the Family Cookvault.
