idea.rationale: Using Canada's legal-deposit rule, which requires every published book to be permanently archived at the national archives and library, to make a supermarket cookbook a permanent record of Quebec family heritage is a smart legislative-loophole-as-medium move. The reframe turns a seasonal corporate gift into a piece of institutional cultural memory.

strategy.rationale: IGA is Sobeys' Quebec grocery banner, deeply tied to Quebecois family-meal culture and one of the province's largest food retailers. Anchoring a holiday gift around heritage Quebec family recipes is direct mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The legal-deposit mechanism is a verifiable institutional fact - Library and Archives Canada genuinely requires this - so the strategic premise is data-backed, although the board does not cite the specific statute.

execution.rationale: The hardcover with embossed red-fabric cover referencing traditional Quebec kitchen tablecloths, the editorial interior layout for each family recipe, and the red-and-white tablecloth-patterned press kit hold together as one publication-design system. Craft on the artefact reads as warm and intentional, but the system is essentially one book plus its launch dressing - it does not extend into a wider identity, signage or retail design canon.

impact.rationale: The board carries no quantified metrics and no press coverage on the extraction, so submission volumes from Quebec families, IGA basket-size lift during the holidays or earned-media reach are not anchored. The legal-deposit framing provides a permanent institutional footprint by definition, which is a real qualitative impact, but it is a structural fact rather than a measured outcome of this specific campaign.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the legal-deposit mechanic is a clever Quebec-specific cultural move and the artefact is well-crafted, but the design system is one book wide and the board offers no metrics to anchor cultural or commercial impact.

one_liner: The back of the canvas IS the disorder.
search_doc:
The back of the canvas IS the disorder.
Mechanisms: two-sided-artefact, craft-as-the-point, book-cover-as-installation, embroidery-as-narrative
Idea: Illustrate the main characters of the book first as polished portrait paintings (the visible face), then collaborate with an artist to embroider the back of each canvas — so the reverse side, exposing the threads, becomes the chaotic 'second face' of the character's mental disorder.
Insight: A book about mental disorders that hide behind a calm face needs a visual identity that literally has two sides — what people see, and what is happening behind it.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DESIGN/BRONZE/The Two Faces - Alexia Ortiz (BBDO Mexico, Mexico City).webp

extracted.tagline: Artworks depicting the two sides of mental disorders.
extracted.context: Some disorders aren't visible, but they eventually take their toll. So, for the launch of "Las dos muertes de Flor González" — a novel that addresses these disorders — we depict it beyond its pages.
extracted.idea: Illustrate the main characters of the book first as polished portrait paintings (the visible face), then collaborate with an artist to embroider the back of each canvas — so the reverse side, exposing the threads, becomes the chaotic 'second face' of the character's mental disorder.
execution: Portrait paintings of the novel's main characters — the visible 'face' of each disorder
Embroidered reverse side of each canvas exposes a tangled-thread 'second face'
Out-of-home posters in cities use the embroidered reverse as the campaign image
Embroidered paintings used as the launch campaign and as the novel's official cover book
metrics: Sold out in just one month
press_coverage: Hipertextual: "The campaign that shows the other face of the embroidered painting"
Andrey González @erikasecundo: "I love it! This is how I had imagined the characters"
Amazon Books: "The cover art of this book is an independent work that awakens an emotion in you"
@enchoseafemenc: "The book alone made me want to read it"

inferred.insight: A book about mental disorders that hide behind a calm face needs a visual identity that literally has two sides — what people see, and what is happening behind it.
inferred.one_liner: The back of the canvas IS the disorder.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframes the novel's cover as a double-sided artwork — embroidered reverse sides become OOH images and the official book cover, helping the title sell out 10,000 copies in a month.

visual.style_description: Dark editorial layout dominated by a row of six paired portraits: a clean painted face beside the chaotic embroidered reverse of the same canvas, six characters in total; below, a strip of OOH placements showing the embroidered portraits on bus shelters and a flat-lay of the printed book; serif headline 'TWO FACES' anchors the top left.

idea.rationale: Painting the polished portrait on one side of the canvas and embroidering the tangled, chaotic reverse to expose the 'second face' of a mental disorder is a precise material idea — the visible/hidden duality is built into the artefact's own front/back geometry rather than implied by the imagery. Strong craft-as-the-point thinking for a literary brief.

strategy.rationale: Alexia Ortiz is a Mexican author whose novel 'Las dos muertes de Flor González' deals with invisible mental disorders, so designing a launch identity built around literal visible/hidden duality is exact brief-fit. The strategic foundation here is the book's own thesis rather than external data — the board does not cite mental-health prevalence figures or research — but for an author-led literary launch the thematic fit is what carries strategic weight.

execution.rationale: The artefacts are well-crafted: six paired portrait-and-embroidered-reverse canvases, then the embroidered reverses extended into OOH bus-shelter placements and used as the official book cover. The system holds across the canvas, OOH and book-cover applications, which is more extension than several other Bronze winners in this batch. The embroidery and painting craft is competent rather than virtuoso, and the typographic system around the work is minimal.

impact.rationale: 10,000 books sold and selling out in one month is the right business outcome for a Mexican literary launch — an absolute count rather than a percentage — but the base context (print run size, market norms for a debut Mexican novel) isn't given on the board, so it's hard to size the achievement. Press coverage on the board is thin and consists largely of social-media quotes rather than substantive critical pickup, which weakens the cultural-impact signal.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the front-vs-back canvas concept and embroidery craft genuinely solve the book's thematic brief and extend across OOH and cover applications, but the impact case rests on a single sales number with no base context.

one_liner: The museum's identity samples the art on its walls.
search_doc:
The museum's identity samples the art on its walls.
Mechanisms: identity-system-redesign, adaptive-colour-system, cultural-institution-rebrand, typography-as-heritage
Idea: Refreshing all art history and its emerging future, the new TMA logomark is a dynamic, multifaceted icon built for the digital age.
Insight: A 123-year-old museum cannot brand itself as one painting — it has to live as a system that adapts to every artwork on the wall.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DESIGN/BRONZE/Toledo Museum Of Art - Toledo Museum Of Art (LaFayette American, Detroit).webp

extracted.context: At over 123 years old, Toledo Museum of Art (TMA) is a beloved cultural institution in Toledo, Ohio and a global leader in the museum field. TMA's brand work leverages Doerge's strengths to create a blueprint for all museums, showing how you can balance different elements: the DNA of an Institution, its physical presence, emerging technologies, etc. The work lifts an unimaginably heavy museum up from the weight of its legacy to face its community, and the world, with a fresh confidence.
extracted.idea: Refreshing all art history and its emerging future, the new TMA logomark is a dynamic, multifaceted icon built for the digital age.
execution: New TMA logomark and 'Toledo Museum of Art' wordmark replacing the previous red-square mark

TMA DNA system: typography draws inspiration from historical styles emphasised by the museum over the years; combination of refined sans serif typeface with a supporting serif typeface used to give flexibility to the new identity
Glass: paying homage to Toledo's history as the City of Glass — colour, transparency and symbolism of glass infused within the identity and applications
Colour: identity expressed through an adaptive colour palette reflecting the artwork within view; two colour fields are sampled, each expanding into three colour-saturation options
Architectural banner system displayed across the museum facade
Wayfinding signage, interior gallery graphics, web and mobile applications
Tote bag and merchandise applications

inferred.insight: A 123-year-old museum cannot brand itself as one painting — it has to live as a system that adapts to every artwork on the wall.
inferred.one_liner: The museum's identity samples the art on its walls.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Replaces a static red-square mark with an adaptive identity that samples colour from the artworks themselves; entrant reports 120% visitation lift, 1140% ticket-sales lift and over 1000% social-engagement growth post-launch.

visual.style_description: Editorial case-study layout on a clean light grey background: left column holds the title, before/after logo lockups and dense body copy on background, challenge, solution and results with metrics; right side stacks application photos — the museum's neoclassical colonnade dressed with primary-coloured banner letters, a red-carpet entrance, gallery interiors, brand-system specimens, merchandise and a magazine-quality fall-foliage exterior.

idea.rationale: Replacing a static red-square mark with an adaptive identity that samples colour from the artworks on the museum's walls is a credible adaptive-identity move for a cultural institution. The mechanism is established in the museum-rebrand canon (Whitney, MoMI, Wexner Center) so the concept is more a confident application than a category-defining reframe.

strategy.rationale: Toledo Museum of Art is a 123-year-old Ohio cultural institution and a recognised global leader in museum practice, with a strong glass-art legacy tied to Toledo's heritage as the 'City of Glass'. Embedding glass symbolism and an adaptive colour palette into the identity is direct mission-fit, anchored in the museum's collection and city history rather than borrowed purpose. The strategic case is built on heritage facts rather than external data points.

execution.rationale: The system extends across logomark, refined sans plus supporting serif, adaptive colour palette, architectural banners on the museum facade, interior gallery wayfinding, web and mobile, and merchandise - a full multi-application identity canon, which is what Design Lions reward at this level. Craft on each surface is competent and disciplined without breaking new ground in typography or art direction; the most distinctive surface is the colonnade banner installation.

impact.rationale: Headline metrics - 120% visitation, 1140% exhibition ticket sales, 116% social impressions and 1105% social engagement - are framed as percentage gains without absolute bases, so '1140% of how many tickets from what baseline?' remains open and the four-digit percentages downplay potentially small starting numbers. The figures are also entrant-reported and not independently audited. The natural business outcome - real attendance and ticket-revenue dollars - is not anchored in absolute terms on the board.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the adaptive-colour identity holds across a full museum application canon with disciplined craft, but the concept is well-trodden in museum branding and the impact metrics are four-digit percentages with no absolute base.

one_liner: Nesting dolls reveal the species hiding inside a tiger.
search_doc:
Nesting dolls reveal the species hiding inside a tiger.
Mechanisms: everyday-object-transformation, purpose-driven-product, illustration-as-system, data-as-artefact
Idea: A collection of nesting dolls (matryoshka) designed as a playful illustrated visual identity for WWF: the outer doll is the umbrella species (tiger, panda, etc.) and the smaller dolls nested inside represent the other species the umbrella species protects.
Insight: According to scientific community, umbrella species are part of the solution: protecting them protects entire ecosystems, but people are unaware that protecting an umbrella species indirectly protects other species in its environment.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DESIGN/GOLD/Umbrella Species - WWF (TBWA_Paris).jpg

extracted.tagline: A smart and playful way to introduce umbrella species.
extracted.context: Every year, more and more animal and plant species are disappearing. The task seems insurmountable and today we no longer even know how to act.
extracted.insight: According to scientific community, umbrella species are part of the solution: protecting them protects entire ecosystems, but people are unaware that protecting an umbrella species indirectly protects other species in its environment.
extracted.idea: A collection of nesting dolls (matryoshka) designed as a playful illustrated visual identity for WWF: the outer doll is the umbrella species (tiger, panda, etc.) and the smaller dolls nested inside represent the other species the umbrella species protects.
execution: Hand-illustrated wooden nesting dolls (matryoshka) sets
Each doll set features one umbrella species (tiger, panda, polar bear, sea turtle, etc.) plus nested dependent species
Sold as merchandise and educational artefact
Companion website and app
Posters and OOH
Social media content featuring influencers

inferred.one_liner: Nesting dolls reveal the species hiding inside a tiger.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A hand-illustrated matryoshka set makes the abstract concept of umbrella species literal and collectible, driving +240% engagement and €1.2M in donations.

visual.style_description: Hero shot of a tall illustrated nesting doll showing a tiger's face on the outermost shell, set inside a clean white display case; a vertical right-hand column shows four more dolls (panda, polar bear, etc.); strong serif headline 'Umbrella Species Dolls' top-left, metric block centred under the hero, and a small thumbnail strip of applications along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Encoding the scientific concept of 'umbrella species' into a nested matryoshka set — the tiger contains the species that depend on it — is a precise visual metaphor that translates a complex ecology principle into a tangible, instantly readable object. It is a rare case of a product that is simultaneously merchandise, infographic, educational toy and identity carrier.

strategy.rationale: WWF is the world's largest conservation NGO and its panda emblem has been the visual shorthand for biodiversity since 1961, so designing an artefact around the umbrella-species concept extends — rather than borrows — the brand's flagship-species heritage. The insight that protecting one umbrella species protects an entire ecosystem is a documented scientific principle credited to Bruce Wilcox and supported by IUCN conservation literature, so the strategic foundation is real rather than rhetorical. The board would benefit from naming the specific science source, but the underlying ecology is verifiable.

execution.rationale: Craft is high and the system is extensible: hand-illustrated wooden dolls across multiple species (tiger, panda, polar bear, sea turtle), a companion app and website, posters, OOH and influencer content all carrying the same illustration language. The decision to use matryoshka — an artefact that is itself about hidden interiority — is materially right for the concept, and the illustration system holds from doll-scale up to poster-scale, which is the Design-jury test.

impact.rationale: The €1,199,118 in donations and 9,742 nesting dolls sold are absolute numbers with clear unit economics, which is the right business outcome for a fundraising-and-awareness NGO product. The '+240% engagement rate' is unanchored — 240% of what baseline? — and should be read as a directional signal rather than a hard outcome. Press footprint on the board is thin (HuffPost only), so the impact case rests primarily on the sales and donation figures.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins Gold because the matryoshka design literalises a scientific principle into a collectible object — the form IS the educational message, not a wrapper around it.
